| FIFA min date      = October 2008

| Elo max date       = August 1927, September 1929, 6 August 1930
| Elo min            = 175
| Elo min date       = August 1995 to May 1996

| pattern_ra1        = _nikechallenge5grw

| pattern_ra2        = _nikechallenge5wb

| rightarm2          = FFFFFF

| socks2             = FFFFFF
| First game         = {{fb|FIN}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|EST}}<br>([[Helsinki]], Finland; 17 October 1920)
| Largest win        = {{fb|EST}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|LTU|1918}}<br>([[Tallinn]], Estonia; 26 July 1928)<br>{{fb|GIB}} 0–6 {{fb-rt|EST}}<br>([[Faro, Portugal|Faro]], Portugal; 7 October 2017)
| Largest loss       = {{fb|FIN}} 10–2 {{fb-rt|EST}}<br>([[Helsinki]], Finland; 11 August 1922)<br>{{fb|GER}} 8–0 {{fb-rt|EST}}<br>([[Mainz]], Germany; 11 June 2019)
| 2ndRegional name      = [[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic Cup]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 27
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[1928 Baltic Cup|1928]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = ''Champions''<br>([[1929 Baltic Cup|1929]], [[1931 Baltic Cup|1931]], [[1938 Baltic Cup|1938]], [[2020 Baltic Cup|2020]], [[2024 Baltic Cup|2024]])
| Current            = 
}}

The '''Estonia men's national football team'''{{efn|{{langx|et|Eesti jalgpallikoondis}}}} ({{langx|et|Eesti Jalgpallikoondis}}) represents [[Estonia]] in international [[association football|football]] matches and is controlled by the [[Estonian Football Association]], the governing body for [[football in Estonia]]. Estonia's home ground is [[Lilleküla Stadium]] in the capital city [[Tallinn]].

The national team's first ever match was held against [[Finland national football team|Finland]] in 1920 and resulted in 6–0 defeat. Estonian footballers have participated only once in the Olympic Games, when they played a single match in the [[Football at the 1924 Summer Olympics|1924 Olympic Games']] final tournament in Paris, France. They were defeated 1–0 by the United States in first round. During World War II, in 1940, Estonia was invaded and occupied by the [[Soviet Union]], and there was no possibility of fielding a national football team again until the country restored full independence in August 1991. After the end of the 1944–1991 Soviet occupation, Estonia's first [[FIFA]]-recognised international match was with [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]] on 3 June 1992, a 1–1 draw at home in Tallinn.

Estonia has never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] or [[UEFA European Championship]]. The team has reached the [[UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying play-offs]], by finishing second in their qualifying group, before being drawn up against [[Republic of Ireland national football team|Ireland]] for a play-off tie, making 2011 the "''[[annus mirabilis]]'' of Estonian football". They have since repeated this feat by qualifying for the  [[UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs]] through being the best group winner in [[2022–23 UEFA Nations League D]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=ERR |first=ERR News {{!}} |date=2023-11-21 |title=Estonia makes Euro 2024 play-offs thanks to Czech Republic win |url=https://news.err.ee/1609171000/estonia-makes-euro-2024-play-offs-thanks-to-czech-republic-win |access-date=2023-12-11 |website=ERR |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Schofield |first=Will |date=2023-11-22 |title=Euro 2024 hopefuls can still reach finals despite not winning a single qualifier |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/football/news/euro-2024-hopefuls-can-still-31498101 |access-date=2023-12-11 |website=The Mirror |language=en}}</ref>

Estonia has also participated in the local sub-regional [[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic Cup]] championship, which takes place every two years between the countries of Estonia, [[Latvia national football team|Latvia]] and [[Lithuania national football team|Lithuania]]. Estonia has won the three-nation Baltic Cup tournament five times — most recently in [[2024 Baltic Cup|2024]] — which is less than either of the other two teams in the tournament, Latvia and Lithuania.

The record for the most international caps by an international is held by [[Konstantin Vassiljev]] with 159. [[Martin Reim]] has 157 caps, who held the European record in 2009 until November of that year. The record for most goals is held by [[Andres Oper]] with 38.

==History==
===The Republic of Estonia (1918–1940)===
Estonians were introduced to the game of football by English sailors in the first years of the 20th century, when the land was still part of the [[Russian Empire]].<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Tallinn: K-Kirjastus, lk 8</ref> The national team was formed after the [[Estonian War of Independence|war of independence]] (1918–1920). It played its first match on 17 October 1920 in Helsinki, Finland which ended in a 6–0 defeat. The game took place on a grass surface, which was a first for the Estonians.<ref>[http://vikerraadio.err.ee/spordiklipp?main_id=3155956&s=start Eesti jalgpallikoondise 1.maavõistlus] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103124622/http://vikerraadio.err.ee/spordiklipp?main_id=3155956&s=start |date=3 November 2013 }} ERR Sport, 17 October 2010</ref> The [[Estonian Football Association]] was founded on 14 December 1921 and affiliated with FIFA in 1923 joining Yugoslavia, Latvia, Poland, Czechoslovakia, Turkey and Uruguay.<ref name="Schwede lk 9">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 9.</ref>

Estonia's only participation in a major tournament took place at the [[Football at the 1924 Summer Olympics|1924 Summer Olympics]] in Paris. Estonians lost their only match in the tournament to the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]] 1–0.<ref name="Olympics 1924">{{Cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/tournaments/archive/tournament=512/edition=197020/matches/match=32294/report.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090205173350/http://www.fifa.com/tournaments/archive/tournament=512/edition=197020/matches/match=32294/report.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=5 February 2009|title=Olympic Football Tournament Paris 1924}}</ref>

The Estonian league season usually lasted from the end of May to September. In 1928 the first [[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic football contest]] was held involving all three nations, it was held nine times during this period.<ref name="Schwede lk 13">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 13.</ref><ref name="RSSSF">{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/baltic-overview.html|title=Baltic Cup Overview|work=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=17 March 2017|archive-date=17 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220717010910/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/baltic-overview.html|url-status=live}}</ref>  Four of them were held in Latvia, two in Estonia and three in Lithuania. Estonia was particularly notable for winning the edition of the tournament in 1938.<ref name="RSSSF"/> In the crucial meeting between them and Latvia at the [[Kadrioru Stadium]], 2,000 out of the 12,000 spectators were Latvians.

Estonia's first [[1934 FIFA World Cup qualification|FIFA World Cup qualifying]] match took place on 11 June 1933 in Stockholm, Sweden, which ended with a Swedish 6–2 win. This match was also the world's first FIFA world cup qualifying match. Since later on Sweden also defeated Lithuania, the match between Estonia and Lithuania was cancelled, because Sweden had already won the group.

Estonia's first points in the FIFA World Cup qualifying rounds were gained in [[1938 FIFA World Cup qualification|1938]], playing the qualification matches in 1937, the third edition of the tournament.<ref name="RSSF 1938">{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/38qual.html|title=WORLD CUP 1938 – QUALIFYING|work=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=17 March 2017|archive-date=20 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220720114857/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/38qual.html|url-status=live}}</ref> At the time teams would play each other once in each group.<ref name="RSSF 1938"/> Estonia were in group one, drawn with Germany, Sweden and Finland. In their first match against Sweden, the team went 2–0 up even before the game reached five minutes of play, only to lose 7–2.<ref name="RSSF 1938"/> This was then followed up with a 1–0 success against Finland in which [[Richard Kuremaa]] scored the only goal of the game in the 56th minute.<ref name="RSSF 1938"/> Qualification was completed with a 4–1 defeat against Germany, despite a goal from [[Georg Siimenson]] taking the teams in at half time with a 1–0 lead for the Estonians.<ref name="RSSF 1938"/><ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 119.</ref> As a result, Estonia failed to qualify for the World Cup.<ref name="RSSF 1938"/>

The team's biggest win came on 26 July 1928 which was a 6–0 success against Lithuania in Tallinn,<ref name="RSSF results">{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablese/est-intres.html|title=Estonia – List of International Matches|work=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=17 March 2017|archive-date=13 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220913092511/https://www.rsssf.org/tablese/est-intres.html|url-status=live}}</ref> meanwhile their biggest defeat came on 11 August 1922 which was a 10–2 loss to Finland.<ref name="RSSF results"/> Out of the team's head coaches before the Second World War, seven of them were Hungarian with Antal Mally taking this position twice. There were four foreign coaches (three Hungarians and one Austrian), while the first Estonian national team was coached by Albert Vollrat in 1932.<ref>Margus Luik, Indrek Schwede. Eesti Jalgpall 2002. Eesti Jalgpalliliidu ametlik teatmik, lk 50</ref> Coaches also played for several seasons, who also determined the composition of the football association.<ref name="Schwede lk 17"/>

Players were mostly in Tallinn clubs, such as [[Tallinna Jalgpalliklubi|TJK]], [[Tallinna Sport|Sport]], [[JK Tallinna Kalev|Kalev]] and Tallinn Estonia.<ref name="Schwede lk 17">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 17.</ref> The republic's most capped players were goalkeeper [[Evald Tipner]] (67) and the outfield players [[Eugen Einmann]] (65), [[Eduard Eelma|Eduard Ellman-Eelma]] (58) and [[Karl-Rudolf Silberg-Sillak]] (52).<ref name="internatsionaalid">{{cite web | title=Kõik Eesti koondislased läbi koondise ajaloo | url=http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/?id=141&keel=ee | publisher=Jalgpallihaigla | language=et | access-date=24 February 2010 | archive-date=3 November 2013 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103140147/http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/?id=141&keel=ee | url-status=dead }}</ref> Top goal scorers were Ellman-Eelma (21 goals in 65 matches), Richard Kuremaa (18/42), [[Arnold Pihlak]] (17/44), Georg Siimenson (14/42) and [[Friedrich Karm]] (9/13).<ref name="väravalööjad">{{cite web | title=Väravalööjate pingerida&nbsp;– Jalgallihaigla | url=http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/?id=138&keel=ee | publisher=Jalgpallihaigla | language=et | access-date=25 February 2010 | archive-date=3 November 2013 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103142817/http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/?id=138&keel=ee | url-status=dead }}</ref> Players received small pay for their contributions&nbsp;– 5 [[Estonian kroon]]i in 1938. The Baltic tournament victory was 50 krooni.

On 18 July 1940 the team played their last official game as an independent nation for more than half a century. The game was played at the Kadrioru Stadium and was a 2–1 victory against Latvia.<ref name="RSSF results"/>

===World War II and Soviet occupation (1940–1991)===
In 1940, the Stalinist Soviet Union [[Estonia in World War II|invaded and occupied]] Estonia and, in August 1940, when Estonia was annexed into the USSR, the national team ceased to exist along with the independent country. During [[German occupation of Estonia during World War II|German occupation]] (1941–1944), the team was revived and they played two unofficial friendlies <!-- with ?? -->(in Riga 0–4 and in Tallinn 1–8), but only few players remained from the pre-war era.<ref name="Schwede lk 31">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 31.</ref>  When Soviet troops invaded Estonia again in 1944, some of the best footballers ([[Richard Kuremaa]], Elmar Tepp, Valter Neeris, etc.) were mobilised; some fled to west. Many ex-nationals ([[Arnold Pihlak]], Arnold Laasner, etc.) were in Estonia's team in [[Geislingen, Zollernalbkreis|Geislingen]]'s refugee camp.<ref name="Schwede lk 31"/>

The clubs were renamed in the second half of the 1940s and the traditions started to fade. According to [[Uno Piir]], the first national team manager after Estonia restored independence in 1991, the reason for football's downfall in Soviet-occupied Estonia was the inability to create a club competitive at the level of the Soviet Union's top clubs, hence the decrease in audience and the favouring of other sports by the governing bodies of sports.<ref name="Schwede lk 32–33">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 32–33.</ref> Formally, the Estonian SSR had its own representative team, but because of the occupation it did not take part of international competitions. Between 1948 and 1976, the [[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic Cup]] was held 19 times, which The Byelorussian SSR won a few times and the Estonian SSR five times.<ref name="RSSSF"/> From 1969 to 1982, Soviet-occupied Estonia was the only Soviet "union republic" not participating in the Soviet Union's football league. During the 1970s, the game lost popularity in Estonia and the sport was mainly played by the Russian immigrant community.<ref name="Schwede lk 32–36">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 32–36.</ref><ref>Reim, Olev (2002). Rahvuskoondise kümme aastat. Lk. 5.</ref>

Estonian football-life was relaunched in mid-70s by the attempts of [[Roman Ubakivi]],<ref name="Schwede lk 33">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 33.</ref> who formed Estonian-language training groups. The most notable team was Lõvid ({{langx|en|Lions}}) in 1980–1989, who were coached by Ubakivi and Olev Reim. Several players, such as [[Mart Poom]] and [[Martin Reim]], became part of the national team later.<ref>Indrek Schwede, Olev Reim: Lõvid on legend, Sporditäht, 1998, nr 12, lk 20–25</ref> Not a single Estonian reached the Soviet national team, but two Ubakivi's pupils, Ott Mõtsnik and [[Toomas Krõm]], broke into the youth team.<ref name="Schwede lk 42">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 42.</ref>

The [[Singing Revolution]], the pursuit to restore Estonian independence and to cool regional tensions, found its way to football as well. On 18 July 1990, an exhibition match was held between Estonian and Latvian footballers at Kadriorg Stadium, to remember the last official match between the two teams as independent nations 50 years previously.<ref name="Schwede lk 47">Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 47.</ref> The principle of assembling the squad was controversial. 63 players made a public addressing ([[Päevaleht]], 24 April 1990) calling out the football governing bodies to only select the descendants of Estonians, leaving out immigrants who came to Estonia after [[World War II]].

===Return to international football (1991–1996)===
Estonia restored its full independence on 20 August 1991, and returned to international football when the team debuted in the [[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic tournament]] which took place in [[Lithuania]] in November 1991.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.rsssf.com/tablesb/baltic|title=Baltic Cup-overview.html Overview}}{{dead link|date=February 2023|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> However, the first recognized match did not take place until June 1992 in [[Tallinn]] as a friendly against [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]] (1–1). This historic meeting under the guidance of coach Uno Piir was overseen by a team consisting of [[Mart Poom]], [[Urmas Hepner]], [[Igor Prins]], [[Urmas Kaljend]], [[Meelis Lindmaa]], [[Toomas Kallaste]], [[Tarmo Linnumäe]], [[Indro Olumets]], [[Martin Reim]], [[Sergei Ratnikov]], [[Risto Kallaste]], [[Viktor Alonen]], [[Urmas Kirs]], [[Marko Kristal]] and [[Aleksandr Puštov]].<ref>Reim, Olev (2002). Rahvuskoondise kümme aastat. pg. 9.</ref> Puštov was the scorer of the Estonian goal.

At that time the composition of the squad was influenced by the country's citizenship policy. There were disputes whether the national team should include players who lived in Estonia but had not acquired Estonian citizenship. Most of those players in question were of Russian origin.<ref>Reim, Olev (2002). Rahvuskoondise kümme aastat. pg. 7–8.</ref><ref>Indrek, Schwede 2001. Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 46–54.</ref> Approximately four months before the first official match against Slovenia, [[FC Flora]] presented the [[Estonian Football Association]] (EFA) an ultimatum signed by 25 players which stated that "only those who have acquired Estonian citizenship on the basis of legal continuity should be included in the national team".<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 48.</ref> In July of the same year FIFA gave the right to represent Estonia to 97 non-citizens who were according to EFA born in Estonia and were in the process of acquiring Estonian citizenship.<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 54.</ref> In October the board of EFA made a decision that after the date of 1 April 1993 non-citizens could no more debut in the national team.<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 53.</ref>

The citizenship dispute heated up again in February 1993 when Estonia took part in a three team friendly tournament held in Finland. For the first time {{citation needed|date=July 2018}}, non-citizen players [[Andrei Borissov]] and [[Sergei Bragin]] were allowed to represent Estonia in the national team. In a statement made on 23 February the government of Estonia urged the [[Estonian Central Sports Union]] to "consider manning Estonian sports teams only with Estonian citizens".<ref name="autogenerated49">Schwede, Indrek 2001. Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 49.</ref> On 11 March the local press published an open letter in which the signatories accused EFA and the head coach [[Uno Piir]] of using four "alien citizens" (Andrei Borissov, Sergei Bragin, Aleksandr Puštov, Sergei Hohlov-Simson) in games and using Russian as the working language of the national team. The signatories also noted that "most of the positions belonging to Estonians (in youth teams) were filled with non-citizens".<ref name="autogenerated49"/> According to Estonian press the EFA had also misled FIFA because most of those 97 players who had gotten the right to represent Estonia had not actually applied for citizenship.

On 5 December 1991 the EFA decided to take part in [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|1994 FIFA World Cup qualification tournament]] despite financial difficulties, the poor state of the Kadrioru Stadium and the inexperience of the national team.<ref>Reim, Olev (2002). Rahvuskoondise kümme aastat. pg. 6–7.</ref> Estonia ended the qualification tournament in the last place of the group and with record of one goal scored and 27 conceded. The team lost nine games and drew once against [[Malta national football team|Malta]].

In the [[UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying]] tournament the team was coached by [[Roman Ubakivi]]. The qualification tournament ended without a single point and a goals record of three scored and 31 against. The biggest defeats came from abroad against [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]] (7–1) and [[Lithuania national football team|Lithuania]] (5–0).

From 14 October 1993 to 5 October 1996 Estonia played without a victory for almost three years and by February 1996 the team had sunk to 135 in the [[FIFA World Rankings]]. Public interest was at a low. In the autumn of 1994 when Estonia hosted [[Italy national football team|Italy]] at the Kadrioru Stadium only 3000 people came to watch.<ref>Schwede, Indrek 2001. Väike jalgpallipiibel. pg. 97.</ref>

===First foreign coach and improved results (1996–2000)===
<!-- Deleted image removed: [[File:Eesti FIFA edetabelis.png|thumb|A rise in the FIFA rankings began after Icelandic coach Teitur Thordarson was appointed.]] -->
Results improved with the arrival of the newly independent team's first foreign coach, Icelander [[Teitur Thordarson]]. His first victory was achieved at the fifth attempt in October 1996, when they defeated Belarus at the Kadriorg Stadium in a [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|1998 World Cup qualifier]] with a goal from Hohlov-Simson. After the victory over the Belarusians, the Estonian team gained infamy on 9 October 1996, when a [[one team in Tallinn|match against Scotland]] had to be rescheduled after the Estonian team failed to turn up for the game.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/scotland-must-play-an-estonian-encore-1351348.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121111184916/http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/scotland-must-play-an-estonian-encore-1351348.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=11 November 2012 |title=Scotland must play an Estonian encore |website=The Independent |date=8 November 1996}}</ref> For unclear reasons, the match was rescheduled to be played on neutral ground in Monaco after it was agreed at a FIFA meeting in Scotland on 7 November, leading to the rescheduled match to take place on 11 February 1997<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.ohtuleht.ee/157551 |title=Šotlased lõõbivad mängu üle, mida kunagi ei toimunud |website=SLÕhtuleht |date=27 May 2004 |access-date=18 July 2011 |archive-date=6 November 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106063153/http://www.ohtuleht.ee/157551 |url-status=live }}</ref> ending in a 0–0 draw. Reasons for the original postponement of the game was that the Scottish team trained at the Kadriorg the night before, finding the [[Floodlights (sport)|floodlighting]] inadequate. This matter was raised with the officials who agreed with their concerns. In protest, the Estonians failed to show up, which kicked off only to be stopped seconds later.

At the end of qualifying, the Estonians finished fifth in a field of six teams on a total of four points ahead of Belarus. This was the first time the Belarusians finished last in a qualifying campaign, and had a weaker goal difference. Estonia scored four goals and conceded sixteen.

Estonia also entered the [[UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying|qualifying tournament for Euro 2000]]. This time round the Estonians recorded three wins and two draws in their group, with fifteen goals scored and seventeen conceded. The team also found themselves in the same group as Scotland, this time losing 3–2 away but drawing 0–0 at home. The Estonian magazine Sporditäht, placed the 1998 events between the pair in their top ten sporting events.<ref>10 tähelepanuväärsemat spordisündmust Eestis, Sporditäht, 1998, nr 12, lk 9</ref>  On 31 March 1999 the Estonians defeated Lithuania 2–1 in Vilnius. Estonia remained a theoretical possibility to qualify for their first major tournament. Despite failing to qualify, they still set themselves a then team record of 11 points. Their meeting with Scotland on 8 September 1999 was a 5,000 sell-out at the Kadriorg.<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 97.</ref>

===A new stadium and the "Dutch period" (2000–2007)===
[[Image:UEFA Euro 2008 Qualifiers - England v Estonia.jpg|thumb|Estonia's national team has had four meetings with [[England national football team|England]], with the last to date being at [[Wembley Stadium]] on 9 October 2015.]]
Head coach Teitur Thordarson resigned at the end of 1999, leading the Estonian football association to look for a new coach. They were taken over this time by [[Tarmo Rüütli]] (who was replaced by caretaker [[Aivar Lillevere]] for two games), who was appointed until autumn 2000, and seen the team through their qualifying group for the 2002 FIFA World Cup. After the departure of Rüütli and Lillevere's two game stint as caretaker manager, the Estonian Football Association made an agreement with Dutchman [[Arno Pijpers]].

Plans were later set by the football association to build a modern football home in Tallinn, which took place in 2000 and construction began outside of the Lilleküla railway line, giving it its original name of the Lilleküla Stadium. The arena opened on 2 June 2001, ahead of their 2002 World Cup qualifying game against the Netherlands (4–2 defeat). The 9,300 tickets on sale for the match sold out within six hours.<ref>Schwede, Indrek (2001). Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 93.</ref>

Their campaign for [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|2002 FIFA World Cup qualification]], saw two victories over Andorra and two draws with Cyprus, which gave the team a total of eight points in the final table and fourth place with ten goals scored and 26 against, finishing ahead of those two teams. This was later matched in [[UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying]], where they gained two more wins over Andorra and draws with Croatia and Bulgaria. The team's goals record was much more stronger defensively, only conceding six goals in their eight matches while scoring four.

Estonia then most successful tournament came in the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|qualifying rounds for the 2006 FIFA World Cup]], under the supervision of Dutchman and assistant coach of Pijpers [[Jelle Goes]], after Pijpers left the post in 2004. Five wins, two draws and five losses gave them 17 points in their qualification group and fourth place. The team were placed ahead of Latvia, Liechtenstein and Luxembourg. [[UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying]] was not as successful, which seen the team finish sixth in a field of seven teams, only ahead of Andorra who were also the only team they recorded wins against and gained a total of seven points. The Estonian FA shortly parted company with Goes in June 2007.

===Rüütli's head coach again and the anniversary year (2008–present)===
[[File:Eesti vs Türgi (0-0) 2008.JPG|thumb|Estonia vs [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]] at the [[Lilleküla Stadium]]. 0–0 draw, 15 October 2008.]]
[[File:Samba boys kick off the match in Tallinn.jpg|thumb|Estonia&nbsp;– [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]] at the Lilleküla Stadium. 1–0 win for Brazil, 12 August 2009.]]
November 2007 saw the approval of a two-year contract for new head coach Tarmo Rüütli,<ref>[https://archive.today/20120907103008/http://www.postimees.ee/231107/esileht/sport/297281.php?tarmo-ruutli-kinnitati-eesti-koondise-peatreeneriks Tarmo Rüütli kinnitati Eesti koondise peatreeneriks] Postimees.ee, 22 November 2007</ref> who had overseen the national team in the 1999–2000 season, with this being the last time the team took on an Estonian coach, as Pijpers was the first of three foreign coaches between 2000 and 2007. Rüütli's main task in his second term was to lead the team through the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|2010 World Cup qualifying]] matches. The team showed volatile form in friendly matches during 2008. In September, the Estonians lost 3–2 to Belgium in an away qualifying match, but fell to a low ebb after being beaten 7–0 by Bosnia also on their travels,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.ohtuleht.ee/295278 |title=Kas Eesti jalgpalli matused? Bosniale kaotati 0:7! |website=SL Õhtuleht |date=10 September 2008 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=6 November 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106062933/http://www.ohtuleht.ee/295278 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://sport.postimees.ee/?id=32503 |title=Eesti jalgpallikoondis jäi häbisse |website=Postimees |date=10 September 2008 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=1 July 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100701045319/http://sport.postimees.ee/?id=32503 |url-status=live }}</ref> and fell to an all-time low of 137th place in the FIFA World Rankings. The first home game of the campaign was a 3–0 loss to Spain, the [[UEFA Euro 2008|reigning European champions]]. The team still picked up points during the qualification, which included holding Euro 2008 semi-finalists Turkey to a 0–0 draw. Further results were a 1–0 win over Armenia, and a 2–2 draw away from home before the campaign was completed with a 2–0 win against the Belgians. The team collected 8 points finishing fifth in a group of six.

2009 was declared the 100th anniversary of Estonian football. The final matches for record cap holder Martin Reim (6 June versus [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]]) and long-standing goalkeeper Mart Poom were held (against [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]] on 10 June). ''Sajandi mäng'' ({{langx|en|Match of the Century}})<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.soccernet.ee/rr-eesti-brasiilia-sajandi-mang-vaevalt-kull |title=Eesti-Brasiilia sajandi mäng? Vaevalt küll |website=Soccernet.ee |date=6 February 2009 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=7 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160307081255/http://soccernet.ee/rr-eesti-brasiilia-sajandi-mang-vaevalt-kull |url-status=live }}</ref> was the first ever match versus [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]], who had arrived in Tallinn as the FIFA World Rankings leaders, and also the five-time world champions, winning 1–0. Much attention was attracted the day after the international friendly, with the Estonian FA announcing that coach Rüütli's contract was to be extended to 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.soccernet.ee/ejl-pikendas-lepingut-tarmo-ruutliga |title=EJL pikendas lepingut Tarmo Rüütliga |website=Soccernet.ee |date=13 August 2009 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=10 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160310225633/http://soccernet.ee/ejl-pikendas-lepingut-tarmo-ruutliga |url-status=live }}</ref>

Estonia later achieved one of its most famous victories, winning 3–1 in a [[UEFA Euro 2012|2012 European Championship]] away qualifier on 8 October 2010 against [[Serbia national football team|Serbia]] then ranked 15th in the FIFA rankings. The match took place four months after the Serbian team had competed in the World Cup.<ref>[http://www.eestisport.ee/section-blog/39-uudised/363-sajandi-voit-eesti-jalgpallikoondis-alistas-voorsil-serbia-31.html SAJANDI VÕIT! Eesti jalgpallikoondis alistas võõrsil Serbia 3:1] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160829063624/http://www.eestisport.ee/section-blog/39-uudised/363-sajandi-voit-eesti-jalgpallikoondis-alistas-voorsil-serbia-31.html |date=29 August 2016 }} eestisport.ee, 8 October 2010</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.uefa.com/uefaeuro/match/2002174--serbia-vs-estonia/ |title=Estonia celebrate their greatest night |website=[[UEFA]] |date=8 October 2010 |access-date=17 March 2011 |archive-date=13 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101113124405/http://en.uefa.com/uefaeuro2012/matches/season=2012/round=15171/match=2002174/postmatch/quotes/index.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

Media attention came from a 2–2 friendly international result with [[Bulgaria national football team|Bulgaria]]. Two days before the friendly match, on 11 February 2011, bets were placed by officials regarding the outcome of the match. Suspicion of match manipulation was raised when a Hungarian referee gave four disputable penalties, being equally distributed between the two sides. The same team of officials also took charge of the game the day before, an international friendly involving [[Latvia national football team|Latvia]] and [[Bolivia national football team|Bolivia]] which ended 2–1 in favour of the Latvians and had also seen three penalties awarded in the game, which were also all of the goals scored.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.soccernet.ee/tulemusega-manipuleerimine-leidis-kinnitust |title=Tulemusega manipuleerimine leidis kinnitust |website=Soccernet.ee |date=11 February 2011 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=22 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160322005116/http://soccernet.ee/tulemusega-manipuleerimine-leidis-kinnitust |url-status=live }}</ref>

The Estonian team got an important victory in their next match, which was on 25 March at the [[Lilleküla Stadium]] over Uruguay in a friendly match. Former World Cup winners Uruguay had recently reached the semi-finals of the 2010 World Cup and were sitting at 7th place in the FIFA rankings at the time of the 2–0 victory. The captain [[Raio Piiroja]] earned his 100th international cap.<ref>[http://www.jalgpall.ee/news.php?st=style_fp.css&news_id=2292 Eesti võitis Uruguayd 2:0!] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181101015652/http://www.jalgpall.ee/news.php?st=style_fp.css&news_id=2292 |date=1 November 2018 }} [[Eesti Jalgpalli Liit]], 25 March 2011</ref> On 29 March, the good performances continued with a 1–1 home draw against Serbia.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.jalgpall.ee/news.php?st=style_fp.css&news_id=2303 |title=Eesti teenis Serbia vastu ülimagusa 1:1 viigi |website=Eesti Jalgpalli Liit |date=29 March 2011 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=30 January 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180130204439/http://www.jalgpall.ee/news.php?st=style_fp.css&news_id=2303 |url-status=live }}</ref>

This was followed by a period of poor form, which began with an unofficial friendly game loss to the [[Basque Country national football team|Basque Country]], qualifying defeats to Italy and the Faroe Islands then followed, before a tour of South America saw the team lose to Chile and Uruguay. A 3–0 loss to Turkey in Istanbul then completed their friendly matches cycle before qualifying resumed. However, the ''Sinisärgid'' won away from home to Slovenia and at home to [[Northern Ireland national football team|Northern Ireland]], which lifted the team to 58th in the FIFA rankings, giving them their best position to date. This win completed their group matches in the Euro 2012 qualifying campaign.

The regular qualification phase for the Euro 2012 tournament was completed with a win in the final game away to Northern Ireland.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://sport.err.ee/jalgpall/9f1cd74c-4072-47ca-a1e4-6f5396a7bcf6 |title=Konstantin Vassiljev tõi kastanid tulest ja Eesti võitis Põhja-Iirimaad 2:1 |website=ERR Sport |date=7 October 2011 |access-date=12 October 2011 |archive-date=3 November 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103124701/http://sport.err.ee/jalgpall/9f1cd74c-4072-47ca-a1e4-6f5396a7bcf6 |url-status=live }}</ref> Four days later, Serbia failed to beat [[Slovenia national football team|Slovenia]], thus Estonia entered a qualifying play-off against the [[Republic of Ireland national football team|Republic of Ireland]] with the first leg in Tallinn. Estonia lost the home game 0–4 but managed a 1–1 draw abroad. The Euro 2012 qualifying campaign was Estonia's best to date, with 16 points achieved out of a possible 30, and was the closest that Estonia came to qualifying for a major tournament.

On 5 June 2012, Estonia set a record for being the first team to have played all of UEFA fellow [[UEFA#Men.27s national teams|52 members]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/news/newsid=1580955.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120414000742/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/news/newsid=1580955.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 April 2012|title=France friendly tees up Estonia's European record|date=8 February 2012|access-date=28 May 2012|language=et}}</ref> Two more sides have been added since 2013 as full UEFA members. Estonia has played the 53rd member, [[Gibraltar national football team|Gibraltar]], but has not yet taken on the newest member, [[Kosovo national football team|Kosovo]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Congress decisions bring Gibraltar on board|url=https://www.uefa.com/news-media/news/0253-0d7eefa3b5d9-668101979b18-1000--congress-decisions-bring-gibraltar-on-board/|publisher=[[UEFA]]|access-date=8 July 2013|date=24 May 2013|archive-date=21 September 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130921054349/http://www.uefa.com/uefa/aboutuefa/organisation/congress/news/newsid=1956133.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Eesti jalgpallikoondis kohtub kahel korral värske UEFA liikmega|url=http://sport.postimees.ee/1269130/eesti-jalgpallikoondis-kohtub-kahel-korral-varske-uefa-liikmega|publisher=[[Postimees]]|access-date=8 July 2013|language=et|date=13 June 2013|trans-title=Estonian national team will meet twice with newest UEFA member|archive-date=15 June 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130615191046/http://sport.postimees.ee/1269130/eesti-jalgpallikoondis-kohtub-kahel-korral-varske-uefa-liikmega|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Kosovo elected to Uefa and can apply for Fifa membership|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/36193445|work=[[BBC News]]|access-date=3 May 2016|date=3 May 2016|archive-date=6 May 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160506031615/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/36193445|url-status=live}}</ref>

On 15 November 2014, Estonia became the first and so far only team to give up a point to [[San Marino national football team|San Marino]] in the European Championship qualifying tournament, when the two sides played to a 0–0 draw in San Marino.

On Tuesday, 28 March 2017, Estonia gained one of the most famous wins in their history as they beat Croatia 3–0 at home in an international friendly.<ref>{{cite web|title=[VIDEO] Estonia Shock Croatia in Tallinn|url=http://www.croatiaweek.com/video-estonia-shock-croatia-in-tallinn/|website=Croatia Week|date=28 March 2017 |access-date=28 March 2017|archive-date=29 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170329153149/http://www.croatiaweek.com/video-estonia-shock-croatia-in-tallinn/|url-status=live}}</ref>

On 19 November 2018, Estonia defeated Greece 1–0 in Athens.

On 10 June 2021, Estonia won the Baltic Cup for the 4th time, beating Latvia 2–1 at home, and it's the first time after 83 years, since 1938.<ref>{{cite web|title=BLOGI JA FOTOD - Eesti jalgpallikoondis võitis Lätit ja tuli 83 aasta järel Balti tšempioniks!|trans-title=BLOG AND PHOTOS - The Estonian football team beat Latvia and became the Baltic champion after 83 years!|url=https://sport.delfi.ee/artikkel/93701769/blogi-ja-fotod-eesti-jalgpallikoondis-voitis-latit-ja-tuli-83-aasta-jarel-balti-tsempioniks|website=sport.delfi.ee|access-date=10 June 2021|date=10 June 2021|language=Estonian|archive-date=10 June 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210610200134/https://sport.delfi.ee/artikkel/93701769/blogi-ja-fotod-eesti-jalgpallikoondis-voitis-latit-ja-tuli-83-aasta-jarel-balti-tsempioniks|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Home stadium==
{{main|Lilleküla Stadium}}
[[File:A. Le Coq Arena - Lilleküla staadion.jpg|thumb|264x264px|[[Lilleküla Stadium]], commonly known as '''A. Le Coq Arena''', has been the national stadium of Estonia since 2001.]]
Home games are played in [[Tallinn]] at the Lilleküla Stadium since 2001, its capacity is about 14,400.<ref name="fcf">{{cite web|title=A. Le Coq Arena|url=https://fcflora.ee/klubi-a-le-coq-arena/|publisher=FC Flora|access-date=30 April 2020|language=et|archive-date=19 April 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220419124548/https://fcflora.ee/klubi-a-le-coq-arena/|url-status=live}}</ref> The stadium borrows its name from its sponsor [[A. Le Coq]], a major Estonian brewery.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.alecoq.ee/est/meietegemised/partnerid/?articleID=287|title=A. Le Coq. Meie partnerid|access-date=12 October 2011|archive-date=25 April 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090425163533/http://www.alecoq.ee/est/meietegemised/partnerid/?articleID=287|url-status=dead}}</ref> The stadium was opened on 2 June 2001, for the sold-out World Cup qualifier versus the Netherlands. This is also Estonia's largest football stadium. Lilleküla Stadium is also the home of [[FC Flora]].

Their previous home ground was the Kadriorg Stadium, which opened in June 1926 with a 3–1 victory over Lithuania. The Kadriorg holds 5,000 seats<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.kadriorustaadion.ee/?id=1340|title=Kadrioru staadion - kadrioru staadion, staadion, jalgpall, kergejõustik, Kadriorg, Kadriorg Stadium|website=www.kadriorustaadion.ee|access-date=12 October 2011|archive-date=8 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180108085543/http://www.kadriorustaadion.ee/?id=1340|url-status=live}}</ref> and in contrast to the Lilleküla Stadium, stages [[athletics (sport)|athletics]] events on a regular basis.
{| class="wikitable sortable" style="margin: 0 1em 1em 0; font-size: 90%;"
|+List of Estonia's home grounds through history:
|- style="background:#0072CE;color:white" 
!Stadium
!Location
!Years
|-
|[[Tiigiveski Ground]]
| rowspan="4" |Tallinn
|1921
|-
|[[Kalevi Aed]]
|1923–1925
|-
|[[Kadriorg Stadium]]
|1926–1940
1992–2000
|-
|[[Lilleküla Stadium]]
|2001–present
|}

Estonia have also staged friendly matches away from Tallinn in [[Kohtla-Järve]], [[Kuressaare]], [[Narva]], [[Pärnu]], [[Rakvere]], [[Tartu]], [[Valga, Estonia|Valga]] and [[Viljandi]].

==Team image==
===Kit===
The kit of the Estonian national team (home games) traditionally consists of a blue shirt, black shorts and white socks, while a change strip (away games), is that of a white shirt, black shorts and blue socks. Before 1996, other colour combinations have been used. The goalkeeper usually wears a yellow jersey, black shorts and yellow socks. The kit [[design]] changes every two years to a new one. Since 1997 the team's supplier has been [[Nike Inc.|Nike]],<ref>{{cite web |url=http://sport.delfi.ee/news/jalgpall/eesti/eesti-koondis-astub-faari-saarte-vastu-uues-vormis.d?id=32547773 |title=Eesti koondis astub Fääri saarte vastu uues vormis |website=Delfi Sport |date=11 August 2010 |access-date=13 October 2011 |archive-date=22 January 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120122050225/http://sport.delfi.ee/news/jalgpall/eesti/eesti-koondis-astub-faari-saarte-vastu-uues-vormis.d?id=32547773 |url-status=dead }}</ref> while between 1992 and 1997 it was supplied by [[Lotto Sport Italia|Lotto]].<ref>[https://archive.today/20120730012505/http://www.epl.ee/artikkel/250154 Eesti vutikoondis saab uuel aastal uued särgid] Eesti Päevaleht, 12 November 2003</ref> Below is a timeline of how the home kit colours have changed through time:

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="width:90%;"
|-
|
{{Football kit box
|pattern_la =
|pattern_b =
|pattern_ra =
|leftarm = ffffff
|body = ffffff
|rightarm = ffffff
|shorts = 4169E1
|socks = 000000
|title = 1922
}}
|
{{Football kit box
|pattern_la =
|pattern_b =
|pattern_ra =
|leftarm = 4169E1
|body = 4169E1
|rightarm = 4169E1
|shorts = ffffff
|socks = 000000
|title = 1924
}}
|
{{Football kit box
|pattern_la =
|pattern_b =
|pattern_ra =
|leftarm = 1E90FF
|body = 1E90FF
|rightarm = 1E90FF
|shorts = 000000
|socks = ffffff
|title = 1992–present
}}
|}

===Supporters===
Estonia's main supporters group of that of the ''Jalgpallihaigla'' (English: ''Football Hospital''), with over 600 members.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/liikmed/|title=Liikmed – Jalgpallihaigla|access-date=27 July 2023|archive-date=27 July 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230727161223/https://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/liikmed/|url-status=live}}</ref> The group is committed to "Deal with all of your supporters issues from ticket distribution in a special fans section, and also with the fans as watchdogs for relations with the Estonian Football Association and their clubs".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/index.php?id=38|title=jalgpallihaigla.ee|access-date=17 March 2017|archive-date=10 November 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111110113700/http://www.jalgpallihaigla.ee/index.php?id=38|url-status=dead}}</ref> Home games see the group as the most vocal, situated in the Southern section of the Lilleküla Stadium.

A busy away journey took place in October 2007, when at Wembley Stadium for the European championship qualifier with England a crowd of two thousand Estonian fans were in attendance.<ref>[http://www.epl.ee/artikkel/403915 Hambutu mäng jättis Eesti fännid tagasihoidlikuks] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080425093330/http://epl.ee/artikkel/403915 |date=25 April 2008 }} EPL, 15 October 2007</ref>

A large number of away fans have visited Tallinn. In 2009 1,700 supporters of Bosnia and Herzegovina were at the Lilleküla Stadium.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.soccernet.ee/nadala-tegija-%E2%80%93-bosnia-bh-fanaticos-fannid |title=Nädala tegija&nbsp;– Bosnia "BH Fanaticos" fännid |website=Soccernet.ee |date=12 October 2009 |access-date=13 October 2011 |archive-date=22 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160322120507/http://soccernet.ee/nadala-tegija-%e2%80%93-bosnia-bh-fanaticos-fannid |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Results and fixtures==
{{Main|Estonia national football team results (2020–present)}}
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

===2026===
{{football box collapsible
|format     = 1
|round      = [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|2026 FIFA Series]]
|date       = 27 March 2026
|time       = 18:00 ([[UTC+02:00|UTC+2]])
|team1      = {{fb-rt|KEN}}

==Players==
{{further|List of Estonia international footballers}}

===Current squad===
The following 25 players were called up to the [[2026 Baltic Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/1-paev-manguni-koondise-koosseisus-toimus-balti-turniiri-finaali-n26060|title=Koondise koosseisus toimus Balti turniiri finaali eel kaks muudatust|publisher=Estonian Football Association|date=8 June 2026|access-date=8 June 2026|language=et}}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of 9 June 2026, after the match against [[Lithuania national football team|Lithuania]].''<ref name="capsandgoals">{{cite news|url=http://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/statistics/view|title=Koondislaste statistika|language=et|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=31 May 2017|archive-date=18 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211018064522/https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/statistics/view|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/friendlies/match/2034802/ | title=Argentina-Estonia &#124; Friendlies &#124; Match &#124; UEFA.com | website=[[UEFA]] | access-date=9 June 2022 | archive-date=9 June 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220609195801/https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/friendlies/match/2034802/ | url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://eu-football.info/_players.php?id=123&data=9|title=Most Estonia Caps - EU-Football.info|website=eu-football.info|access-date=9 June 2022|archive-date=9 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220609195518/https://eu-football.info/_players.php?id=123&data=9|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up to the squad within the last twelve months.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/meeste-koondise-koosseisus-toimusid-muudatused-n26010|title=Meeste koondise koosseisus toimusid muudatused|publisher=Estonian Football Association|date=31 May 2026|access-date=31 May 2026|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/jurgen-henn-avalikustas-koosseisu-balti-turniiriks-n25992|title=Jürgen Henn avalikustas koosseisu Balti turniiriks|publisher=Estonian Football Association|date=27 May 2026|access-date=26 May 2026|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/martin-vetkal-ei-osale-fifa-series-turniiril-n25780|title=Martin Vetkal ei osale FIFA Series turniiril|date=26 March 2026|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=26 March 2026|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/jarelvaadatav-jurgen-henn-teatas-koosseisu-mangudeks-norra-ja-n25373|title=Jürgen Henn teatas koosseisu mängudeks Norra ja Küprosega|date=6 November 2025|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=6 November 2025|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/uudised/selgusid-eesti-ja-andorra-koosseisud-tanaseks-manguks-n25135|title=Selgusid Eesti ja Andorra koosseisud tänaseks mänguks|date=9 September 2025|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=9 September 2025|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/uudised/jurgen-henn-teatas-koosseisu-mangudeks-itaalia-ja-andorraga-n25067|title=Jürgen Henn teatas koosseisu mängudeks Itaalia ja Andorraga|date=26 August 2025|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=26 August 2025|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/ejl/uudised/rasmus-peetson-jaab-mangudest-iisraeli-ja-norraga-eemale-n24787|title=Rasmus Peetson jääb mängudest Iisraeli ja Norraga eemale|date=4 June 2025|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=4 June 2025|language=et}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://jalgpall.ee/ejl/uudised/jarelvaadatav-selgus-eesti-koondise-koosseis-aasta-esimesteks-n24742|title=Selgus Eesti koondise koosseis aasta esimesteks kodumängudeks|date=27 May 2025|publisher=Estonian Football Association|access-date=27 May 2025|language=et}}</ref>

{{nat fs r player|no=16|pos=FW|caps=3|goals=0|name=[[Marten-Chris Paalberg]]|age={{birth date and age|2008|10|8|df=y}}|club=[[AS Saint-Étienne|Saint-Étienne]]|clubnat=FRA|latest=v. {{fb|CYP}}, 18 November 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|caps=4|goals=1|name=[[Danil Kuraksin]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|4|4|df=y}}|club=[[FC Flora|Flora]]|clubnat=EST|latest=v. {{fb|MDA}}, 14 October 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|caps=0|goals=0|name=[[Karel Eerme]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|4|22|df=y}}|club=[[Harju JK Laagri|Harju]]|clubnat=EST|latest=v. {{fb|AND}}, 9 September 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury<br />
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby<br />
<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team<br />
<sup>SUS</sup> Serving suspension<br />
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
{{updated|9 June 2026.}}<ref>{{cite web|title=Koondislaste statistika|url=https://jalgpall.ee/koondis/1/statistics/view|language=et|publisher=Estonian Football Association|accessdate=9 June 2026}}</ref>

:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Estonia.''

|}

===Olympic Games===
{{main|Estonia at the 1924 Summer Olympics}}
[[Image:Estonia1924.jpg|thumb|Estonia national team at the [[Football at the 1924 Summer Olympics|1924 Summer Olympics]]]]
Estonia's only participation in a major tournament was at the [[Football at the 1924 Summer Olympics|1924 Summer Olympics]] in [[Paris]], France. Coached by Hungarian [[Ferenc Kónya]], Estonia's participation was limited to a single match in the first round as the team lost 0–1 to the [[United States men's national soccer team|United States]], with [[Andy Straden]] scoring the winning goal from the penalty spot in the 15th minute. Estonia were also given a penalty and a chance to equalise, but [[Elmar Kaljot]]'s effort struck the crossbar in the 68th minute. After going out of the tournament, the Estonian team stayed on in Paris for three weeks, playing a friendly match against [[Republic of Ireland national football team|Ireland]], which ended in a 1–3 defeat, and then went to Germany, playing friendly matches against various teams including a 2–2 draw against [[1. FC Kaiserslautern]].<ref name="Schwede lk 11–12">Schwede, Indrek 2001. Väike jalgpallipiibel. Lk 11–12.</ref>

===Baltic Cup===
{{main|Baltic Cup (football)}}

===Milestones===
* First World Cup qualification game: 11 June 1933, [[Stockholm]], [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]] (6–2 loss) (first FIFA World Cup qualification match in history);
* First World Cup victory and also first away win: 19 August 1937, [[Turku]], Finland (1–0);
* First European Championship qualifying game: 4 September 1994, [[Tallinn]], Croatia (2–0 loss);
* First World Cup victory since return to independence: 5 October 1996, Tallinn, Belarus (1–0);
* First European Championship victory: 4 June 1998, Tallinn, Faroe Islands (5–0);
* First away win in the European Championship: 31 March 1999, [[Vilnius]], Lithuania (2–1).

==Honours==
===Regional===
* '''[[Baltic Cup (football)|Baltic Cup]]'''
** '''Champions (6)''': [[1929 Baltic Cup|1929]], [[1931 Baltic Cup|1931]], [[1938 Baltic Cup|1938]], [[2020 Baltic Cup|2020]], [[2024 Baltic Cup|2024]], [[2026 Baltic Cup|2026]]
** Runners-up (6): [[1928 Baltic Cup|1928]], [[1936 Baltic Cup|1936]], [[1937 Baltic Cup|1937]], [[1993 Baltic Cup|1993]], [[1996 Baltic Cup|1996]], [[2018 Baltic Cup|2018]]
** Third place (16): [[1930 Baltic Cup|1930]], [[1932 Baltic Cup|1932]], [[1935 Baltic Cup|1935]], [[1991 Baltic Cup|1991]], [[1992 Baltic Cup|1992]], [[1994 Baltic Cup|1994]], [[1995 Baltic Cup|1995]], [[1997 Baltic Cup|1997]], [[1998 Baltic Cup|1998]], [[2001 Baltic Cup|2001]], [[2003 Baltic Cup|2003]], [[2008 Baltic Cup|2008]], [[2010 Baltic Cup|2010]], [[2012 Baltic Cup|2012]], [[2016 Baltic Cup|2016]], [[2022 Baltic Cup|2022]]
'''Friendly'''
* '''[[FIFA Series]]'''
**{{Silver2}} '''Runner-Up (1):''' [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)|2026 FIFA Series]]<ref name="2026 FIFA Series result">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 30, 2026 |title=New Zealand net four as quartet win shootouts |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/articles/fifa-series-matchday-six-round-up |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 6, 2026}}</ref>
